sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage:
A research team investigates glycolytic regulation in hepatocytes under varying metabolic conditions. They measure the concentrations of glycolytic intermediates and the activities of key regulatory enzymes. Under high-energy conditions (elevated ATP, citrate, and glucose-6-phosphate), they observe significant accumulation of fructose-6-phosphate (F6P) with minimal fructose-1,6-bisphosphate (F1,6BP) production.

The three key regulatory enzymes of glycolysis are:
• Hexokinase/glucokinase (glucose → glucose-6-phosphate)
• Phosphofructokinase-1, PFK-1 (fructose-6-phosphate → fructose-1,6-bisphosphate)
• Pyruvate kinase (phosphoenolpyruvate → pyruvate)

The team notes that liver cells express glucokinase rather than hexokinase. Unlike hexokinase, glucokinase has a high Km for glucose (~10 mM) and is not inhibited by its product glucose-6-phosphate.
questions.stem: The accumulation of F6P under high-energy conditions most directly indicates inhibition of which enzyme, and by which effector?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hexokinase, inhibited by glucose-6-phosphate

choices.label: B
choices.text: PFK-1, inhibited by ATP and citrate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pyruvate kinase, inhibited by ATP

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphoglucose isomerase, inhibited by fructose-6-phosphate

explanations.A: The passage specifies that liver cells express glucokinase, not hexokinase, and glucokinase is not inhibited by glucose-6-phosphate. Even in cells expressing hexokinase, product inhibition by G6P would cause accumulation of glucose, not F6P. F6P accumulation points to a block downstream of F6P production but upstream of F1,6BP production.
explanations.B: Correct. F6P accumulates because the enzyme that consumes it — PFK-1 — is inhibited. PFK-1 is the committed step of glycolysis and is allosterically inhibited by ATP (signaling energy abundance) and citrate (signaling active TCA cycle). Both are elevated under the high-energy conditions described. When PFK-1 is inhibited, its substrate F6P builds up while its product F1,6BP is depleted. PFK-1 is the most important regulatory point in glycolysis because it catalyzes the first committed step — the irreversible phosphorylation that commits the carbon skeleton to glycolytic breakdown rather than alternative pathways like glycogen synthesis or the pentose phosphate pathway.
explanations.C: While pyruvate kinase is also inhibited by ATP (and by alanine in the liver), its inhibition would cause accumulation of phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP), not fructose-6-phosphate. PEP is several steps downstream of F6P. Identifying which intermediate accumulates tells you exactly which enzyme is blocked — the intermediate accumulates immediately upstream of the inhibited step.
explanations.D: Phosphoglucose isomerase catalyzes the reversible interconversion of G6P and F6P. It is a near-equilibrium enzyme that is not a significant regulatory point in glycolysis. Enzymes at near-equilibrium are not typically subject to allosteric regulation because their activity is governed primarily by substrate and product concentrations, not by effector molecules.

questions.stem: During glycolysis, ATP is produced by substrate-level phosphorylation at two steps: the phosphoglycerate kinase reaction and the pyruvate kinase reaction. Which of the following correctly distinguishes substrate-level phosphorylation from oxidative phosphorylation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Substrate-level phosphorylation requires oxygen; oxidative phosphorylation does not

choices.label: B
choices.text: Substrate-level phosphorylation transfers a phosphate group directly from a substrate to ADP; oxidative phosphorylation uses a proton gradient to drive ATP synthase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Substrate-level phosphorylation occurs only in the mitochondria; oxidative phosphorylation occurs in the cytoplasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Substrate-level phosphorylation produces more ATP per glucose molecule than oxidative phosphorylation

explanations.A: This reverses the oxygen requirement. Substrate-level phosphorylation does not require oxygen — it occurs in glycolysis, which proceeds under both aerobic and anaerobic conditions. Oxidative phosphorylation is the process that requires oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor in the electron transport chain. The name 'oxidative' refers to this oxygen dependence.
explanations.B: Correct. In substrate-level phosphorylation, a high-energy phosphate group is transferred directly from a phosphorylated substrate intermediate to ADP, forming ATP. This occurs at specific enzymatic steps: phosphoglycerate kinase (1,3-BPG → 3-PG) and pyruvate kinase (PEP → pyruvate) in glycolysis, and succinyl-CoA synthetase in the TCA cycle (which produces GTP). No membrane, no gradient, no electron transport is involved. In oxidative phosphorylation, the energy from NADH and FADH₂ oxidation drives proton pumping across the inner mitochondrial membrane, creating an electrochemical gradient. Protons flow back through ATP synthase (Complex V), which harnesses this chemiosmotic energy to phosphorylate ADP to ATP.
explanations.C: This reverses the compartmentalization. Substrate-level phosphorylation occurs in the cytoplasm (glycolysis) and the mitochondrial matrix (TCA cycle). Oxidative phosphorylation occurs at the inner mitochondrial membrane. The locations are functionally significant: glycolytic substrate-level phosphorylation in the cytoplasm provides ATP even without mitochondria, which is critical for mature red blood cells that lack mitochondria entirely.
explanations.D: The opposite is true. Per glucose molecule, substrate-level phosphorylation produces only 4 ATP (net 2 from glycolysis + 2 GTP from TCA cycle). Oxidative phosphorylation produces approximately 30–32 ATP from the NADH and FADH₂ generated by glycolysis, pyruvate dehydrogenase, and the TCA cycle. Oxidative phosphorylation is far more efficient, which is why aerobic organisms extract dramatically more energy from glucose than anaerobic organisms.

questions.stem: The passage states that liver cells express glucokinase (Km ≈ 10 mM) rather than hexokinase (Km ≈ 0.1 mM). The normal blood glucose concentration is approximately 5 mM. What is the physiological significance of glucokinase's high Km?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glucokinase is always fully saturated at physiological glucose levels, ensuring constant hepatic glucose uptake

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucokinase activity is proportional to blood glucose concentration over the physiological range, allowing the liver to act as a glucose buffer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glucokinase has higher catalytic efficiency than hexokinase, enabling faster glucose processing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glucokinase's high Km prevents the liver from metabolizing glucose, reserving it for other tissues

explanations.A: At a blood glucose concentration of 5 mM and a Km of 10 mM, glucokinase is operating at [S]/Km = 0.5 — well below saturation. Using Michaelis-Menten: V₀ = Vmax × 5/(10+5) = Vmax/3, meaning glucokinase is only about 33% saturated at normal blood glucose. If it were fully saturated, its activity would not change with blood glucose levels, defeating its regulatory purpose.
explanations.B: Correct. Because glucokinase's Km (10 mM) is above normal blood glucose (5 mM), the enzyme operates in the first-order region of its kinetic curve — the region where velocity increases nearly linearly with substrate concentration. When blood glucose rises after a meal (to 8–12 mM), glucokinase activity increases proportionally, driving increased hepatic glucose uptake and glycogen synthesis. When blood glucose falls during fasting, glucokinase activity drops proportionally, reducing hepatic glucose consumption. This makes the liver a glucose buffer that absorbs excess glucose after meals and reduces uptake during fasting, helping to stabilize blood glucose. By contrast, hexokinase (Km = 0.1 mM) is >98% saturated at 5 mM glucose, so its rate barely changes with blood glucose — it runs at near-constant velocity, appropriate for tissues like the brain that need steady glucose consumption regardless of blood levels.
explanations.C: Catalytic efficiency (kcat/Km) is not determined by Km alone. A high Km actually reduces catalytic efficiency (all else being equal) because the enzyme requires higher substrate concentrations to achieve significant rates. Glucokinase's advantage is regulatory, not catalytic — its glucose-responsive activity enables metabolic flexibility.
explanations.D: The liver does metabolize glucose, particularly after meals when blood glucose is high. Glucokinase's high Km doesn't prevent glucose metabolism — it makes the rate of glucose metabolism proportional to glucose availability. After a carbohydrate-rich meal, glucokinase is quite active, driving glycogen synthesis and other glucose-utilizing pathways.

questions.passage:
A biochemist traces the metabolic fate of a uniformly ¹⁴C-labeled acetyl-CoA molecule entering the TCA cycle. The acetyl-CoA (2 carbons) condenses with oxaloacetate (4 carbons) to form citrate (6 carbons). Over the course of one complete turn of the cycle, two carbon atoms are released as CO₂ — at the isocitrate dehydrogenase step (isocitrate → α-ketoglutarate) and at the α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase step (α-ketoglutarate → succinyl-CoA).

Importantly, the two carbons released as CO₂ in the first turn are derived from the oxaloacetate portion of citrate, not from the incoming acetyl group. The labeled carbons from acetyl-CoA are redistributed within the cycle intermediates and are released as CO₂ in subsequent turns.
questions.stem: Based on the passage, if a researcher adds a single pulse of uniformly ¹⁴C-labeled acetyl-CoA to a mitochondrial preparation actively running the TCA cycle, when would ¹⁴C first appear in CO₂?

choices.label: A
choices.text: During the first turn of the cycle, at the isocitrate dehydrogenase step

choices.label: B
choices.text: During the first turn of the cycle, at the α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase step

choices.label: C
choices.text: During the second turn of the cycle

choices.label: D
choices.text: During the third turn of the cycle, after the labeled carbons have been fully equilibrated

explanations.A: The passage explicitly states that the two CO₂ molecules released in the first turn are derived from the oxaloacetate portion of citrate, not from the incoming acetyl group. Therefore, ¹⁴C from labeled acetyl-CoA would not appear in CO₂ at this step during the first turn.
explanations.B: Same reasoning as above — both CO₂-releasing steps in the first turn (isocitrate dehydrogenase and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase) release carbons from oxaloacetate, not from the newly entered acetyl-CoA. This is a common misconception: students often assume that since 2 carbons enter as acetyl-CoA and 2 leave as CO₂, the same atoms exit. The molecular geometry of citrate makes this impossible in the first turn.
explanations.C: Correct. The ¹⁴C-labeled carbons from acetyl-CoA are incorporated into the four-carbon intermediates (succinate, fumarate, malate, oxaloacetate) during the first turn but are not released as CO₂. In the second turn, when this now-labeled oxaloacetate condenses with a new acetyl-CoA and proceeds through the cycle, the labeled carbons occupy positions that can be released as CO₂ at the decarboxylation steps. Due to the symmetric nature of succinate and fumarate (which scrambles the label between the two carboxyl groups), some ¹⁴C will appear in CO₂ during the second turn, with more released in subsequent turns. Complete release of all labeled carbon requires multiple turns.
explanations.D: While complete release of all ¹⁴C does require many turns (due to the randomization at symmetric intermediates), some ¹⁴C will appear in CO₂ during the second turn, not the third. The question asks when ¹⁴C first appears, not when it is completely released.

questions.stem: For each acetyl-CoA that enters the TCA cycle, the cycle produces a specific set of reduced coenzymes and GTP. Which of the following correctly states the per-acetyl-CoA yield?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 ATP

choices.label: B
choices.text: 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 GTP

choices.label: C
choices.text: 4 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 GTP

choices.label: D
choices.text: 3 NADH, 2 FADH₂, 1 ATP

explanations.A: This undercounts NADH by one. There are three NADH-producing steps in the TCA cycle, not two. Additionally, the TCA cycle produces GTP (via succinyl-CoA synthetase), not ATP directly, although GTP and ATP are energetically equivalent and interconvertible.
explanations.B: Correct. Per acetyl-CoA, one turn of the TCA cycle produces: 3 NADH (at isocitrate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and malate dehydrogenase), 1 FADH₂ (at succinate dehydrogenase, which is also Complex II of the electron transport chain), and 1 GTP (at succinyl-CoA synthetase, via substrate-level phosphorylation). Since each glucose produces 2 acetyl-CoA (via pyruvate dehydrogenase), the per-glucose TCA yield is 6 NADH, 2 FADH₂, and 2 GTP. Adding pyruvate dehydrogenase's contribution (1 NADH per pyruvate, 2 per glucose) gives a total of 8 NADH, 2 FADH₂, and 2 GTP from pyruvate oxidation through the TCA cycle.
explanations.C: Four NADH per turn would overcount. There are exactly three oxidation steps in the TCA cycle that use NAD⁺ as the electron acceptor. You may be inadvertently including the pyruvate dehydrogenase reaction (pyruvate → acetyl-CoA), which produces 1 NADH but is not technically part of the TCA cycle — it is a connecting reaction that precedes cycle entry.
explanations.D: This overcounts FADH₂ (only 1, not 2) and misidentifies the nucleotide triphosphate produced. The single FADH₂ is produced at succinate dehydrogenase. The substrate-level phosphorylation step (succinyl-CoA synthetase) produces GTP in most mammalian tissues, though some tissues have isoforms that produce ATP. For the MCAT, GTP is the standard answer.

questions.stem: Succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II) is unique among TCA cycle enzymes because it is embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane and directly feeds electrons into the electron transport chain. Why does this reaction produce FADH₂ rather than NADH?

choices.label: A
choices.text: FAD is a stronger oxidizing agent than NAD⁺, and the succinate-to-fumarate oxidation does not release enough free energy to reduce NAD⁺

choices.label: B
choices.text: FADH₂ is more stable than NADH in the hydrophobic membrane environment

choices.label: C
choices.text: FAD is covalently bound to the enzyme, while NAD⁺ would need to diffuse freely

choices.label: D
choices.text: FADH₂ can donate electrons directly to coenzyme Q, whereas NADH cannot interact with the membrane

explanations.A: Correct. The oxidation of succinate to fumarate (converting a C–C single bond to a C=C double bond) releases relatively little free energy compared to other TCA cycle oxidations. This reaction is not thermodynamically favorable enough to reduce NAD⁺ to NADH, because NAD⁺ is a weaker oxidizing agent that requires a larger free energy release from the substrate to accept electrons. FAD, being a stronger oxidizing agent, can accept electrons from this less energetically favorable oxidation. Put simply, the succinate → fumarate reaction does not release enough energy to ‘push’ electrons onto NAD⁺, but it releases enough to reduce FAD. This thermodynamic matching between substrate and electron carrier is why different dehydrogenases use different cofactors — each enzyme is paired with the cofactor that is energetically compatible with its specific reaction. Consequently, FADH₂ enters the ETC at Complex II (lower in the chain than Complex I where NADH enters), pumps fewer protons, and yields fewer ATP (~1.5 vs ~2.5 per molecule).
explanations.B: While membrane compatibility is a consideration for Complex II's structure, it is not the reason FAD is used instead of NAD⁺. NADH is produced by other enzymes that interact with the membrane (e.g., NADH is oxidized at Complex I, which is membrane-embedded). The choice of cofactor is driven by thermodynamic constraints on the redox reaction, not by membrane solubility.
explanations.C: While FAD is indeed covalently bound to succinate dehydrogenase (as a prosthetic group rather than a freely diffusing cosubstrate), this structural feature is a consequence of the enzyme's design, not the thermodynamic reason for using FAD. Other flavoproteins use covalently bound FAD for reactions where NAD⁺ cannot serve as the electron acceptor due to energetic constraints.
explanations.D: NADH does interact with the inner mitochondrial membrane — it donates electrons to Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase), which is membrane-embedded. The claim that NADH cannot interact with the membrane is factually incorrect. The reason succinate dehydrogenase uses FAD is thermodynamic: the succinate oxidation does not release enough energy to reduce NAD⁺.

questions.passage:
Researchers study the effects of various compounds on oxidative phosphorylation using isolated mitochondria. They measure oxygen consumption rate and ATP production rate simultaneously.

Compound A: Oxygen consumption continues at a high rate, but ATP production drops to near zero. The proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane dissipates.

Compound B: Both oxygen consumption and ATP production cease. The proton gradient increases beyond normal levels.

Compound C: Both oxygen consumption and ATP production cease. The proton gradient dissipates to zero.

The researchers also note that Compound A causes a significant increase in heat production by the mitochondria.
questions.stem: Based on the experimental observations, Compound A most likely functions as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: An electron transport chain inhibitor that blocks electron flow at Complex III

choices.label: B
choices.text: An ATP synthase inhibitor that blocks the proton channel of Complex V

choices.label: C
choices.text: An uncoupling agent that makes the inner mitochondrial membrane permeable to protons

choices.label: D
choices.text: A competitive inhibitor of NADH dehydrogenase at Complex I

explanations.A: An ETC inhibitor would block electron flow, preventing both oxygen consumption (O₂ is the terminal electron acceptor) and proton pumping. This would cause oxygen consumption to cease, but Compound A shows continued high oxygen consumption. ETC inhibitors cause electrons to pile up upstream of the block, halting the entire chain.
explanations.B: An ATP synthase inhibitor (like oligomycin) blocks the proton channel through Complex V. Protons cannot flow back into the matrix, so the gradient builds up. The elevated gradient eventually inhibits the ETC (backpressure effect), slowing both proton pumping and electron transport, which reduces oxygen consumption. Compound A shows continued oxygen consumption and a dissipated gradient — the opposite of an ATP synthase inhibitor.
explanations.C: Correct. Compound A is an uncoupler (like 2,4-dinitrophenol/DNP or the physiological uncoupling protein thermogenin/UCP1). Uncouplers carry protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane, bypassing ATP synthase. This dissipates the proton gradient, so ATP synthase has no driving force and ATP production stops. However, the ETC is now uninhibited — in fact, without the backpressure of a proton gradient, the ETC runs at maximum speed, consuming oxygen rapidly while pumping protons that immediately leak back. The energy that would normally drive ATP synthesis is instead released as heat (explaining the increased heat production). This is the physiological basis of nonshivering thermogenesis in brown adipose tissue, where UCP1 deliberately uncouples oxidative phosphorylation to generate warmth.
explanations.D: A Complex I inhibitor (like rotenone) would block electron entry from NADH, reducing oxygen consumption. While electrons from FADH₂ could still enter at Complex II, the overall oxygen consumption rate would decrease, not increase. Compound A shows high oxygen consumption, ruling out ETC inhibition at any complex.

questions.stem: Compound B causes both oxygen consumption and ATP production to cease while the proton gradient increases. Which compound's mechanism is most consistent with these observations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cyanide, which binds the iron in Complex IV

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oligomycin, which blocks the proton channel of ATP synthase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rotenone, which blocks electron transfer at Complex I

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antimycin A, which blocks electron transfer at Complex III

explanations.A: Cyanide inhibits Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) by binding to the iron-containing heme group, preventing the final transfer of electrons to O₂. This would halt oxygen consumption (correct) and eventually halt ATP production (correct). However, with the ETC completely blocked, no proton pumping occurs, so the proton gradient would dissipate over time, not increase. The increased gradient specifically indicates that protons are still being pumped but cannot flow back — pointing to a block at ATP synthase, not the ETC.
explanations.B: Correct. Oligomycin binds to the Fₒ subunit of ATP synthase (Complex V), physically blocking the proton channel. Protons that have been pumped into the intermembrane space cannot return to the matrix. This causes the proton gradient to build up beyond normal levels. The excessive gradient creates thermodynamic backpressure on the ETC: pumping additional protons against the steep gradient becomes energetically unfavorable, so electron transport slows and eventually stops. When electron transport stops, oxygen is no longer consumed. ATP production ceases because the proton channel through which flux must occur is physically blocked. The key distinguishing observation is the increased gradient — this uniquely identifies an ATP synthase inhibitor.
explanations.C: Rotenone blocks electron transfer from the iron-sulfur clusters of Complex I to coenzyme Q, halting NADH oxidation. This would stop oxygen consumption (partially — FADH₂ can still enter at Complex II) and reduce ATP production. However, without proton pumping from Complex I, the gradient would not increase. Rotenone's effects are more similar to Compound C's profile (gradient dissipation) than Compound B's (gradient increase).
explanations.D: Antimycin A blocks electron flow at Complex III (cytochrome bc₁) between cytochrome b and cytochrome c₁. This halts the entire ETC downstream of Complex III, stopping oxygen consumption and proton pumping at Complexes III and IV. Without continued proton pumping, the gradient cannot increase. Antimycin A is an ETC inhibitor, not an ATP synthase inhibitor.

questions.stem: According to the chemiosmotic hypothesis, approximately how many protons must flow through ATP synthase to produce one molecule of ATP, and what provides the energy for this process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1 proton; the energy comes from NADH oxidation directly at ATP synthase

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2 protons; the energy comes from the electrical potential across the membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: 4 protons; the energy comes from the electrochemical proton gradient (proton-motive force) across the inner mitochondrial membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: 10 protons; the energy comes from substrate-level phosphorylation coupled to the membrane

explanations.A: NADH is oxidized at Complex I, not at ATP synthase. ATP synthase does not directly interact with NADH. The energy coupling in oxidative phosphorylation is indirect (chemiosmotic): the ETC uses NADH oxidation energy to pump protons, and ATP synthase uses the resulting proton gradient to make ATP. Peter Mitchell received the Nobel Prize for this insight, which overturned the earlier hypothesis that a direct chemical intermediate coupled the ETC to ATP synthesis.
explanations.B: The electrical potential (Δψ) across the membrane is one component of the proton-motive force, but the full driving force also includes the chemical concentration gradient (ΔpH). Together, these constitute the electrochemical gradient. Additionally, current estimates suggest approximately 4 protons per ATP (based on the c-ring stoichiometry of mammalian ATP synthase), not 2.
explanations.C: Correct. Current structural evidence suggests mammalian ATP synthase has a c-ring of 8 subunits, and one full rotation (requiring 8 protons through the F₀ channel) produces 3 ATP at the F₁ catalytic head, giving approximately 8/3 ≈ 2.7 H⁺ per ATP through ATP synthase itself. However, one additional proton is consumed to transport Pᵢ into the matrix via the phosphate carrier (H₂PO₄⁻/H⁺ symport), bringing the effective total to approximately 4 H⁺ per ATP when transport costs are included. This is the standard MCAT value and accounts for the full chemiosmotic cost of ATP synthesis and export. The proton-motive force (pmf = Δψ − 2.3RT/F × ΔpH) combines both the electrical potential difference (Δψ ≈ 150–180 mV, inside negative) and the pH gradient (ΔpH ≈ 0.5–1.0 units, matrix more alkaline). Both components drive protons through ATP synthase’s F₀ channel, causing rotation of the c-ring and the attached γ-subunit, which induces conformational changes in the F₁ catalytic head that drive ATP synthesis.
explanations.D: Ten protons per ATP is too high and would imply a very inefficient coupling ratio. Substrate-level phosphorylation is a mechanistically distinct process from chemiosmotic ATP synthesis — it does not involve the membrane or a proton gradient. The two mechanisms of ATP production are independent and should not be conflated.

questions.passage:
During prolonged fasting, the body increasingly relies on fatty acid β-oxidation for energy production. A nutritional biochemist studies the oxidation of palmitoyl-CoA (16 carbons) in liver mitochondria. β-oxidation proceeds through repeating cycles of four reactions: oxidation (producing FADH₂), hydration, oxidation (producing NADH), and thiolysis (releasing acetyl-CoA).

Each cycle shortens the fatty acyl chain by 2 carbons and produces 1 FADH₂, 1 NADH, and 1 acetyl-CoA. The final cycle produces 2 acetyl-CoA molecules from the last 4-carbon intermediate (butyryl-CoA → 2 acetyl-CoA with 1 FADH₂ and 1 NADH).
questions.stem: How many total acetyl-CoA molecules and how many cycles of β-oxidation are required to completely oxidize palmitoyl-CoA (C16)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 8 acetyl-CoA from 8 cycles

choices.label: B
choices.text: 8 acetyl-CoA from 7 cycles

choices.label: C
choices.text: 7 acetyl-CoA from 7 cycles

choices.label: D
choices.text: 16 acetyl-CoA from 8 cycles

explanations.A: While 8 acetyl-CoA is correct (16 carbons ÷ 2 carbons per acetyl-CoA = 8), 8 cycles is not. The final cycle cleaves a 4-carbon intermediate into two acetyl-CoA molecules, so the last cleavage produces the 7th and 8th acetyl-CoA simultaneously. This means only 7 cleavage cycles are needed, not 8.
explanations.B: Correct. A 16-carbon fatty acid produces 16/2 = 8 acetyl-CoA molecules. However, the number of β-oxidation cycles is (n/2) − 1 = (16/2) − 1 = 7 cycles. This is because each cycle removes one acetyl-CoA from the chain, but the final cycle splits a 4-carbon unit into two acetyl-CoA molecules simultaneously. After 7 cycles: 7 FADH₂ + 7 NADH from β-oxidation, plus 8 acetyl-CoA that each enter the TCA cycle to produce 3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP. Totals: 31 NADH (×2.5 ATP = 77.5), 15 FADH₂ (×1.5 ATP = 22.5), and 8 GTP, giving approximately 108 ATP from palmitoyl-CoA. Subtracting the 2 ATP equivalents consumed during activation of free palmitate to palmitoyl-CoA (ATP → AMP + PPᵢ, equivalent to 2 ATP → 2 ADP), the net yield from palmitate is approximately 106 ATP — far more per molecule than glucose (30–32 ATP).
explanations.C: Seven cycles is correct, but 7 acetyl-CoA is wrong. The final cycle of β-oxidation cleaves butyryl-CoA (4 carbons) into two acetyl-CoA molecules, not one. Therefore, 7 cycles produce 8 acetyl-CoA (6 from the first 6 cycles + 2 from the final cycle).
explanations.D: A 16-carbon fatty acid cannot produce 16 acetyl-CoA molecules. Each acetyl-CoA contains 2 carbons, so the maximum from 16 carbons is 16/2 = 8. You may have confused the number of carbons with the number of acetyl-CoA units.

questions.stem: The researcher observes that adding malonyl-CoA to the mitochondrial preparation inhibits β-oxidation. This occurs because malonyl-CoA inhibits:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thiolase, the enzyme that cleaves acetyl-CoA from the fatty acid chain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (CPT-I), the enzyme that transports fatty acyl-CoA into the mitochondrial matrix

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acyl-CoA dehydrogenase, the enzyme that catalyzes the first oxidation step

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase, the enzyme that initiates fatty acid synthesis

explanations.A: Thiolase (β-ketothiolase) catalyzes the final step of each β-oxidation cycle, cleaving the β-ketoacyl-CoA to release acetyl-CoA. It is not the target of malonyl-CoA inhibition. Inhibiting thiolase would block a specific step within β-oxidation, but the physiological regulatory mechanism operates at the entry point to control overall flux.
explanations.B: Correct. Malonyl-CoA is the first committed intermediate in fatty acid synthesis (produced by acetyl-CoA carboxylase). Its accumulation signals that the cell is in a fed state and actively synthesizing fatty acids. Malonyl-CoA allosterically inhibits CPT-I, the outer mitochondrial membrane enzyme that conjugates fatty acyl-CoA to carnitine for transport across the inner membrane. By blocking mitochondrial entry of fatty acids, malonyl-CoA prevents the futile cycle of simultaneously synthesizing and degrading fatty acids. This is a elegant example of reciprocal metabolic regulation: an intermediate of the anabolic pathway (synthesis) inhibits the catabolic pathway (oxidation) at its gateway step.
explanations.C: Acyl-CoA dehydrogenase catalyzes the first reaction within each β-oxidation cycle (producing a trans-Δ² double bond and FADH₂). While deficiencies in this enzyme family (e.g., MCAD deficiency) cause clinical disorders, it is not the physiological target of malonyl-CoA regulation.
explanations.D: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC) is the enzyme that produces malonyl-CoA from acetyl-CoA, not the enzyme inhibited by malonyl-CoA. ACC is itself regulated — activated by citrate and insulin signaling, inhibited by AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) and glucagon signaling. Inhibiting ACC would decrease malonyl-CoA production, which would relieve CPT-I inhibition and promote β-oxidation — the opposite of what the question describes.

questions.stem: Compared to glucose, fatty acids yield more ATP per gram. Which property of fatty acids best explains their higher energy density?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fatty acids have more phosphate groups available for substrate-level phosphorylation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fatty acid carbons are more reduced (bonded to more hydrogens) than glucose carbons, providing more electrons for oxidative phosphorylation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fatty acids are metabolized more quickly than glucose, producing ATP at a faster rate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fatty acids bypass the TCA cycle entirely, delivering all electrons directly to the ETC

explanations.A: Fatty acids contain no phosphate groups. Neither glycolysis nor β-oxidation uses phosphate groups intrinsic to the fatty acid substrate for substrate-level phosphorylation. ATP production from fatty acids is overwhelmingly through oxidative phosphorylation of the NADH and FADH₂ generated during β-oxidation and the TCA cycle.
explanations.B: Correct. The key is oxidation state. In a fatty acid like palmitate (CH₃(CH₂)₁₄COOH), most carbons are bonded to 2-3 hydrogens and carry an average oxidation state of approximately −2. In glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), each carbon has an average oxidation state of 0 (it is already partially oxidized by the oxygen atoms). More reduced carbons can donate more electrons during oxidation — each C-H bond that is oxidized to C=O or C-OH yields electron pairs that ultimately reduce NAD⁺ or FAD. Palmitate (C16) produces 7 FADH₂ + 7 NADH from β-oxidation and 24 NADH + 8 FADH₂ + 8 GTP from 8 turns of the TCA cycle. This enormous electron harvest from highly reduced carbons is why fats store approximately 9 kcal/g compared to 4 kcal/g for carbohydrates.
explanations.C: Speed of metabolism (ATP per unit time) is a question of metabolic flux, not energy density (ATP per gram). In fact, glucose can produce ATP faster than fatty acids in some contexts — anaerobic glycolysis is extremely rapid but inefficient. Energy density depends on the total ATP yield per gram of substrate, which is determined by the oxidation state of the substrate carbons, not the speed of their metabolism.
explanations.D: Fatty acids do not bypass the TCA cycle. After β-oxidation, the acetyl-CoA produced enters the TCA cycle, which is the major source of NADH from fatty acid metabolism. Without the TCA cycle, the ATP yield from fatty acids would be dramatically lower — β-oxidation alone accounts for less than 40% of the total electron harvest from palmitoyl-CoA.

questions.stem: Gluconeogenesis converts pyruvate to glucose using a pathway that is largely the reverse of glycolysis. However, three glycolytic steps are irreversible and must be bypassed by different enzymes. Which of the following correctly pairs a glycolytic enzyme with its gluconeogenic bypass?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phosphofructokinase-1 is bypassed by phosphoglucose isomerase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hexokinase is bypassed by glucose-6-phosphatase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pyruvate kinase is bypassed by pyruvate dehydrogenase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phosphoglycerate kinase is bypassed by phosphoglycerate mutase

explanations.A: Phosphoglucose isomerase catalyzes the reversible interconversion of G6P and F6P — it functions in both glycolysis and gluconeogenesis and is not a bypass enzyme. The gluconeogenic bypass for PFK-1 is fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (FBPase-1), which hydrolyzes F1,6BP to F6P, removing the phosphate that PFK-1 added. PFK-1 and FBPase-1 are reciprocally regulated to prevent futile cycling.
explanations.B: Correct. The three irreversible glycolytic steps and their gluconeogenic bypasses are: (1) Hexokinase/glucokinase (glucose → G6P) is bypassed by glucose-6-phosphatase (G6P → glucose + Pi). This final step of gluconeogenesis occurs in the ER of liver and kidney cells, releasing free glucose into the blood. (2) PFK-1 (F6P → F1,6BP) is bypassed by fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase. (3) Pyruvate kinase (PEP → pyruvate) is bypassed by the combined action of pyruvate carboxylase (pyruvate → oxaloacetate, requiring biotin and ATP) and PEP carboxykinase (oxaloacetate → PEP + CO₂, requiring GTP). The remaining seven steps of glycolysis are near-equilibrium and run in reverse during gluconeogenesis using the same enzymes.
explanations.C: Pyruvate dehydrogenase converts pyruvate to acetyl-CoA — this is an irreversible oxidative decarboxylation that feeds into the TCA cycle, not a gluconeogenic enzyme. In fact, because pyruvate dehydrogenase is irreversible, animals cannot convert acetyl-CoA (and therefore fatty acids) back into glucose. The actual bypass for pyruvate kinase uses two enzymes: pyruvate carboxylase and PEP carboxykinase.
explanations.D: Phosphoglycerate kinase and phosphoglycerate mutase both catalyze near-equilibrium reactions in glycolysis. Neither requires a bypass enzyme for gluconeogenesis — they simply run in the reverse direction when substrate concentrations favor it. Only the three highly exergonic (irreversible) steps of glycolysis require bypass enzymes in gluconeogenesis.

questions.stem: The pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) diverges from glycolysis at glucose-6-phosphate. Which of the following correctly states the two major products of the oxidative phase of the PPP and their primary biological functions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NADH and ribose-5-phosphate; NADH for oxidative phosphorylation and ribose-5-phosphate for glycolysis

choices.label: B
choices.text: NADPH and ribulose-5-phosphate; NADPH for fatty acid synthesis and ribulose-5-phosphate for the Calvin cycle

choices.label: C
choices.text: NADPH and ribose-5-phosphate; NADPH for reductive biosynthesis and antioxidant defense, and ribose-5-phosphate for nucleotide synthesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: FADH₂ and erythrose-4-phosphate; FADH₂ for the electron transport chain and erythrose-4-phosphate for amino acid synthesis

explanations.A: The PPP produces NADPH, not NADH. This single-letter distinction is biologically critical: NADPH (carrying electrons for reductive biosynthesis) and NADH (carrying electrons for energy production via the ETC) have different metabolic roles despite similar structures. NADPH has an extra phosphate group on the 2'-hydroxyl of the adenosine ribose, which allows enzymes to discriminate between the two. Ribose-5-phosphate is used for nucleotide synthesis, not fed back into glycolysis (though the non-oxidative phase can interconvert PPP intermediates with glycolytic intermediates).
explanations.B: NADPH is correct, but the oxidative phase directly produces ribulose-5-phosphate, not ribose-5-phosphate. Ribulose-5-phosphate is then converted to ribose-5-phosphate by an isomerase in the non-oxidative phase. This distinction matters because ribulose-5-phosphate sits at a branch point: it can be isomerized to ribose-5-phosphate (for nucleotide synthesis) or epimerized to xylulose-5-phosphate (which enters the non-oxidative rearrangement reactions). The Calvin cycle reference is also misplaced — it is a plant pathway for carbon fixation in photosynthesis, not a human metabolic pathway.
explanations.C: Correct. The oxidative phase of the PPP produces 2 NADPH (at glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase and 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase) and 1 ribulose-5-phosphate per glucose-6-phosphate. Ribulose-5-phosphate is then isomerized to ribose-5-phosphate in the non-oxidative phase. NADPH serves two critical functions: (1) reductive biosynthesis — providing electrons for fatty acid synthesis, cholesterol synthesis, and amino acid metabolism; (2) antioxidant defense — NADPH is required by glutathione reductase to regenerate reduced glutathione (GSH), which protects cells from oxidative damage. This is especially important in red blood cells, where G6PD deficiency (the most common enzyme deficiency worldwide) impairs NADPH production and causes hemolytic anemia upon oxidative stress. Ribose-5-phosphate is the sugar backbone of nucleotides (ATP, GTP, DNA, RNA) and is essential for cell division.
explanations.D: The PPP does not produce FADH₂. Erythrose-4-phosphate is an intermediate of the non-oxidative phase, not a major product of the oxidative phase. While erythrose-4-phosphate participates in aromatic amino acid synthesis (via the shikimate pathway), this occurs in plants and microorganisms, not in humans.

questions.stem: During the absorptive (fed) state following a carbohydrate-rich meal, insulin levels are high and glucagon levels are low. Which of the following combinations of metabolic pathway activities is most consistent with the fed state in the liver?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Glycolysis active, gluconeogenesis active, glycogenolysis active, β-oxidation active

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glycolysis active, glycogenesis active, fatty acid synthesis active, pentose phosphate pathway active

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gluconeogenesis active, glycogenolysis active, β-oxidation active, ketogenesis active

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glycolysis active, glycogenesis active, gluconeogenesis active, fatty acid synthesis active

explanations.A: This combination includes contradictory pathways. Glycolysis and gluconeogenesis are reciprocally regulated — both being active simultaneously would create a futile cycle, wasting ATP without net metabolic work. Similarly, glycogenolysis (glycogen breakdown) is a fasted-state pathway activated by glucagon, not insulin. β-oxidation is also a fasted-state pathway inhibited in the fed state by malonyl-CoA.
explanations.B: Correct. In the fed state, insulin promotes anabolic (building) pathways and suppresses catabolic (breakdown) pathways. In the liver: Glycolysis is active — glucose is abundant, PFK-1 is activated by insulin-mediated signaling and by fructose-2,6-bisphosphate (whose levels increase when insulin activates PFK-2). Glycogenesis (glycogen synthesis) is active — insulin activates glycogen synthase via protein phosphatase 1 and the PP1/GSK3 signaling cascade, storing excess glucose as glycogen. Fatty acid synthesis is active — excess acetyl-CoA from glycolysis is converted to fatty acids by acetyl-CoA carboxylase (activated by citrate and insulin signaling) and fatty acid synthase. The PPP is active — providing NADPH for fatty acid synthesis and ribose-5-phosphate for nucleotide synthesis in the anabolic state.
explanations.C: This describes the fasted state, not the fed state. Gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis, β-oxidation, and ketogenesis are all activated by glucagon and low insulin — they mobilize stored fuels and produce glucose and ketone bodies to sustain the brain and other tissues during fasting. This is the metabolic opposite of the fed state.
explanations.D: Glycolysis, glycogenesis, and fatty acid synthesis are all appropriately active in the fed state. However, gluconeogenesis is a fasted-state pathway that is suppressed by insulin and would not be active alongside glycolysis. Running glycolysis and gluconeogenesis simultaneously would constitute a futile cycle (glucose → pyruvate → glucose) with net ATP consumption and no productive metabolic work.
